macro_segments.title: The Prohibition Against Freeing Slaves and Exceptions for Transgressions

micro_segments.title: The teaching of Rav Yehuda in the name of Shmuel: One who frees his slave violates a positive commandment
Abaye's position: Freeing a slave to prevent sexual abuse overrides the positive commandment
Distinction between a fully enslaved woman and a half-slave: forced freedom only in extreme cases
Challenge from the story of Rabbi Eliezer freeing his slave to complete a minyan
Debate between Rabbi Yishmael (permission) and Rabbi Akiva (obligation not to free slaves)
Three ways wealthy people lose their wealth: freeing slaves, inspecting property on Shabbat, and lavish meals

macro_segments.title: Methods of Freeing a Slave: Sanctification, Renouncing Ownership, and Personal Status Changes

micro_segments.title: Sanctifying one's slave (makdish) causes him to go free: Rashi's explanation of automatic upgrade of holiness
Renouncing ownership (mafkir) of a slave causes him to go free
Question: Does freedom from property ownership automatically change personal status or require a writ of manumission (get shichrur)?
Rav Chiya bar Avin in the name of Rav: Both sanctification and renouncing ownership require a get shichrur for personal status change
Debate over sanctified property with slaves: Can treasurers (gizbarim) free them or only sell them?
Rashi's interpretation: Sanctification means accepting personal obligation to pay value, not transferring ownership to Temple
Rabbi Meir's principle: One does not speak words for naught; reinterpreting statements to find valid meaning

macro_segments.title: Holiness of Sanctified Slaves and Meila (Misuse of Sacred Property)

micro_segments.title: All agree sanctified slave is holy; debate concerns whether meila applies (land-like) or not (chattel-like)
Special case of hair ready to be shorn: Does readiness for cutting determine if it is like land (no meila) or chattel (meila applies)?

macro_segments.title: Holiness of Sanctified Slaves and Meila (Misuse of Sacred Property) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Parallel case: Ripe grapes ready to harvest versus growing hair; Rashi/Tosafot explain when something is considered detached

macro_segments.title: Challenge to Rav's Position and the Case of a Deceased Convert's Slaves

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef reports Rav's teaching: mafkir (renounce ownership) requires a get shichrur for personal status change
Rabbi Yochanan agrees: mafkir requires get shichrur
Case of convert who dies with no heirs: adult slaves take ownership of themselves (kanu atzman b'nei chorin)
Abba Shaul: Minor slaves cannot take possession of themselves; anyone seizing them acquires them
Problem: If get shichrur is required but convert is dead, who can write it? Answer via analogy to divorced woman (ger k'ishto)
Master-slave relationship persists as personal status even after property ownership ends; only master can grant freedom
Case: Master renounces ownership (mafkir) then dies; does slave auto-free or remain stuck in limbo status?
Ameimar's ruling: Slave remains stuck; needs writ but no one alive to grant it; works for self but not fully free in personal status
Ruling: We follow Abba Shaul regarding minor slaves in convert's estate

topical_tags.term: Positive commandment not to free slaves
Sexual abuse as grounds for freeing
Rava's three ways wealth is lost
Rav Chiya bar Avin amar Rav
Rabbi Meir: Ein adam motzi devarav l'vatala
Hair ready to be shorn (se'ar omed ligzoz)
Kanu atzman b'nei chorin (slaves take ownership of themselves)
